blocks.content: Millhaven premiered at a small documentary festival in Austin on a Thursday in March.
Kit's mother flew in and sat in the third row in a green dress without saying a word. When the lights came up she remained seated — hands in her lap, eyes straight ahead.
Then she turned to Daisy, who was seated beside her, and said:
Daisy took her hand. Held it.
In the Q&A afterward, someone asked what the film was really about. He looked out at the audience, then said:

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: What happens to love when it doesn't have anywhere to go. And whether it turns into something else, or whether it just — stays. Waiting.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: The narration isn't mine. My wife wrote it. And she's a better writer than I'll ever be, so the true parts — those are hers.
Daisy, in the third row, looked at her lap and smiled.
The film won the jury prize. Kit accepted, thanked the festival, and said one more thing:

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: This is for my mom. Who stayed.
In the third row, Ruth Jenkins pressed two fingers to her lips.
